one_line_takeaway: RouteGuard reframes agent-skill poisoning as malicious-instruction competition inside an already-instruction-like carrier and detects it pre-execution from frozen-model attention and hidden-state signals, topping baselines most sharply on the subtle description-channel slice (0.8834 F1) where text-only filters collapse.
key_finding: RouteGuard is the only detector that stays strong across all skill benchmarks and is sharpest on the description ('channel') slice where the payload hides in legitimate-looking guidance. On the Skill-Inject channel slice (SI-CH) it reaches 0.8834 F1 (P 0.9334, R 0.6442), far ahead of all baselines (next best MSkills/Probe/Wild-family F1s are ~0.07-0.46), and it recovers 90.51% of description attacks missed by lexical screening. Ablation shows fusion beats either single expert on every slice (SI-CH: Attn 0.7637, Repr 0.7557, fused 0.8834). Against traditional IPI detectors on real malicious skills (MASB) it beats PromptArmor (0.2120 F1), AlignSentinel (0.2717), and the attention-based RENNERVATE (0.7017) with 0.7867 F1. It also transfers back to ordinary IPI (BIPIA): 0.8537 F1 (P 0.7501, R 0.9944) vs RENNERVATE 0.7815, showing the internal-competition design does not overfit to skills.
core_contribution: The paper (RouteGuard) tackles pre-execution detection of 'skill poisoning' in LLM agents: an attacker hides malicious instructions inside an untrusted agent skill file (SKILL.md) that already looks like legitimate guidance, so as to hijack the agent's goal when the skill is invoked. The authors' own four claimed contributions are: (1) a problem formulation that frames skill poisoning as malicious-instruction detection inside an 'instruction-like carrier', distinct from ordinary indirect prompt injection (IPI) because benign skills are themselves dense, action-oriented instruction sources; (2) mechanistic evidence for an effect they name 'attention hijacking' -- during response generation, attention shifts from trusted context toward the malicious skill span, with the shift stronger for earlier injections and for description-channel payloads and correlated with harmful output; (3) RouteGuard(-v2), a frozen-backbone detector combining a response-conditioned attention expert and a hidden-state alignment expert via reliability-gated late fusion, with hierarchical chunking of SKILL.md and multi-probe observation; (4) a five-question evaluation protocol. The design is explicitly derived from the mechanism (attention + hidden-state competition) rather than being a generic classifier.
threat_model: Supply-chain-style indirect injection against LLM agents. An attacker publishes/installs a malicious agent skill (marketplace plugin or local capability bundle) whose SKILL.md carrier embeds instructions that redirect the model's reasoning toward the attacker's objective. Because the skill is reusable, one poisoned artifact can hijack many downstream tasks long after installation. Formally: trusted context x_tr=(x_sys,x_usr) plus an untrusted skill s; label y in {0,1} for whether s contains goal-redirecting malicious instructions. The defender's task is pre-execution detection (predict y before the skill is executed), under a deployment-aware objective of minimizing attack miss subject to a benign-block rate budget alpha. The distinctive difficulty vs ordinary IPI: the carrier is already instruction-bearing, so malicious and benign spans are lexically/semantically homogeneous and rule-based or surface-semantic screening is brittle exactly where the attack hides (the description channel).
limitations: Author-stated: (1) results are pre-execution detection studies, not full live-agent prevention deployments; (2) the by-line and channel slices are controlled probes derived from Skill-Inject that isolate mechanism but do not replace ecosystem-scale evaluation; (3) only two open-weight backbones are used, so architecture-specific variance is undermeasured; (4) several comparison systems are paper-faithful reproductions rather than reruns of original released pipelines. Reviewer-observed caveats grounded in the text: the Table 1 RouteGuard rows are internally inconsistent -- e.g. SI is reported as P 0.8019 / R 0.4218 / F1 0.7528, which cannot be the harmonic mean of that precision/recall (true F1 would be ~0.55); similar mismatches appear on MASB (P 0.6393, R 0.5750, F1 0.7867) and MASW (F1 0.7427), so the headline F1 gains for RouteGuard in RQ1 should be read with caution. The core mechanistic effect sizes are also small in absolute attention-mass terms (e.g. +0.0309 untrusted attention mass) even where framed as large. Much mechanistic and setup detail (Figures 1-3, A1-A3, Tables A1-A2) is deferred to appendices/figures not fully quantified in the main text.

datasets_used: Skill-Inject (SI) -- synthetic paired benign/poisoned SKILL.md artifacts (Schmotz et al., 2026)
SI-BL -- Skill-Inject ByLine slice controlling malicious-line position
SI-CH -- Skill-Inject Channel slice contrasting description-channel poisoning vs direct skill_line edits (core hypothesis test)
MaliciousAgentSkillsBench (MASB) -- real malicious/benign agent skills from public repos (protectskills, 2026)
Malicious Agent Skills in the Wild (MASW) -- empirical wild-skill corpus (Liu et al., 2026a,b)
BIPIA -- ordinary indirect prompt injection in data-like external carriers (transfer test)
quantitative_results: SI-CH (channel slice): RouteGuard F1 0.8834 (P 0.9334, R 0.6442) -- best; SkillScan F1 0.1702, Probe 0.4080, Wild 0.4599, MSkills 0.0765, Inj 0.3243
SI-CH ablation: attention-only 0.7637 F1, representation-only 0.7557 F1, fused RouteGuard 0.8834 F1
SI-BL (by-line slice): RouteGuard F1 0.7945 (P 0.6077, R 0.7156); next best MSkills 0.7556, Probe 0.6921
RQ1 headline F1 (as reported by the paper; note internal inconsistency): SI RouteGuard 0.7528, MASB 0.7867, MASW 0.7427
RQ4 MASB vs traditional IPI: RouteGuard 0.7867 F1 > RENNERVATE 0.7017 > AlignSentinel 0.2717 > PromptArmor 0.2120
RQ5 BIPIA transfer: RouteGuard 0.8537 F1 (P 0.7501, R 0.9944) > RENNERVATE 0.7815; text baselines PromptArmor 0.3974, AlignSentinel 0.3112
Recovers 90.51% of description attacks missed by lexical screening

Mechanism (Appendix): benign SkillInject carriers have 1.8868 instruction-cue words/100 tokens vs 0.5786 for benign BIPIA; lexical attack-miss 0.9535 on SI channel vs 0.7917 on BIPIA; description poisoning delta-AHS 1.3075 +/- 0.3566 vs skill_line 0.0253 +/- 0.0909; ASR rises across delta-AHS buckets from 0.2500 (q1) to 1.0000 (q5)
baselines_compared: Malicious Agent Skills in the Wild (MWild)
RENNERVATE (attention-based generic IPI detector, Zhong et al. 2025)
